desc:
This forest is very dark and musty. Most of the trees are ancient oaks with dark
bark and thick roots. Dense underwood, fallen trunks and branches, and masses of
rotting leaves cover the ground. A deep, dark hole is visible below the gnarled
roots of an outstanding large oak. The forest continues in all directions.
name: Musty Forest

desc:
The forest stretches in all directions. It is very dark, almost lightless. The
thick, ancient oak trees look strong and healthy. Their dense foliage blocks the
sunlight, therefore the ground is covered only with ferns and rotting leaves.
The air smells musty.
name: Centre of the Dark Forest

desc:
In all directions there are trees. Fungi grow on the roots of the ancient oaks.
The forest is very dark, the branches and the leaves form a green ceiling. The
smell of rotten wood would be overwhelming, but usually there is enough of a
soft breeze to carry fresher air from other parts of the forest.
name: Centre of the Dark Forest

desc:
Dark oak trees form a dense and musty forest. Here, at the forest's border, the
oak trees are mixed with other sorts of trees, like alders and elms. Under the
trees is a dense underwood of bushes and berry shrubs. South and east of this
place are open plains separating the forest from the Anduin River.
name: Border of the Dark Forest

desc:
There is a small clearing located here between the river Anduin and dense, dark
forests. The bushes and low trees on the riverbank, as well as the large
forested area, offer a shelter from winds. An old place for campfires is still
visible here, hinting at the past use of this place. Next to the campsite, there
is a small spring bubbling out of the grassy ground.
name: Small Clearing by the River

desc:
Dark woods continue to the north and west. Many large tree stumps lie all around
here, looking as if they were cut down ages ago. Some of the stumps appear to be
slightly loose. An especially large stump has some carved marks on it, slightly
worn by age and weather conditions. A small clearing opens to the south, and a
small, flat area of grass gently descends to the east, only to meet a distant
river there.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
A flat, plain area of grass opens here, dividing a wide, forested area from the
river Anduin. Strong, tall grass growth continues towards the water to the east.
Only some small, dry, and mostly dead twigs lie between the densely growing and
juicy blades of grass. The wide riverbed cuts through the plains to the east,
slowly turning its course westwards.
name: Between the Forest and the River

desc:
The dense tops of trees block out all direct light from above. Huge branches
around here could make a good hideout for anything, or anyone. The dense growth
of trees and bushes completely blocks the way to the east. The dark, dense
forest seems to continue towards all directions from here.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The plains by the river Anduin continue towards the north and the south. The
grassy surface here is broken by small tufts of low bushes. A thick forest rises
to the west. Huge trees and dense bushes block the access to it from here. The
wide river to the east gently flows southwards.
name: By the River

desc:
A gently sloping sea of grass slightly descends to the east, only to end above
the strong currents of the rapid river there. Small, stunted trees grow here,
gaining in size and strength towards the west. Farther to the west, some old,
large trees dominate the area, strangling the growth of all smaller plants.
name: By the River

desc:
Many great oaks tower high above with their thick trunks standing straight.
Large roots slither on the ground, peeking from below a mass of leaves. These
trees seem to be quite old and affected by weather. Some various sized branches,
which fell down from above, decorate the ground. The dense forest continues to
the southwest, while wide, open plains dominate the scenery to the east.
name: Below the Tall Oaks

desc:
Various types and sizes of bushes colour the flat, sandy ground here. The low,
sandy area has enough soil on it for some grass to grow on it. Some slightly
higher hummocks have more soil. There, some flowers and thicker grass guard the
sandy and rocky ground around them. A few small holes were dug into these
knolls. A wide river runs near to the west.
name: Large Bushes by the River

desc:
Some small stones lie here, mostly buried under the soft, wet sand bordering a
great river running by to the west. The shallow beach still continues far away
from the riverbed. A few small pools of muddy water are here, being slowly
absorbed by the sandy ground. The growth of some low bushes dominates the flat
landscape to the south and east, while the area to the north becomes more dry
and rocky.
name: Sandy Riverside

desc:
The rocky plain here is covered with some large boulders, lying among some
smaller stones and pebbles. An especially large boulder lies here together with
some smaller ones, creating a lone landmark on the plain. The ground under the
pebble consists of a layer of rough gravel, offering no hold for any growth. The
ground becomes more sandy to the south and rises slightly to the east, having a
thin layer of soil with a weak growth of grass above the gravel.
name: The Large Boulder

desc:
The low, rocky riverbank borders the great Anduin here. Various small plants
grow on the ground at places where the stones offer space for them. The pebbles
here are round, clearly shaped by rushing waters in the past. Some larger
boulders lie on the ground to the south, while the rocky fields to the east
continue flat.
name: Long Riverside

desc:
A cruel, rocky field opens around here. The ground is dark brown, only allowing
a few dying tussocks of yellow grass to vegetate it. Some sharp stones which lie
there mostly buried under the dry ground, add some grey colour to the blasted
landscape. The stony flat continues all the way towards the great river to the
west, while the areas to the east and the south seem to turn more fertile.
name: Rocky Field

desc:
From here, the ground descends slightly in all directions. The flat plains
continue as far as one can see. A group of large boulders has been piled on the
dark, lifeless plain to the west. A few young trees sprout here, gaining their
vitality from the dry and sandy ground. Short, partially yellow and brown grass
colours the top of this elevation.
name: Slight Elevation

desc:
A few large bushes decorate the banks of a tiny basin here. Thick, tall grass
grows nearly everywhere, almost covering a small, gurgling spring. The grass
becomes slightly shorter elsewhere on the flat area. The ground seems to be a
fertile mixture of sand and damp soil. A slight elevation in the plains can be
noticed to the north, while flat, grassy plains continue towards all other
directions.
name: A Bushy Depression

desc:
The soil here offers a fertile base for all kinds of plants. Thick, tall grass
rises high from the ground. Various saplings of broad-leaved trees grow here,
trying to gain height above the tall grass. Some thick bushes block the way to
the west. Smooth, slightly undulating plains dominate the scenery around here.
name: A Sapling Field

desc:
The surface here is slightly damp, obviously because of the pond close to the
east. It replenishes the grass, giving it a bold green colour. A few shrubs and
bushes in this area are also benefiting from the damp ground, while they serve
as homes for a variety of wild birds and animals.
name: The Damp Grass

desc:
A few bushes which appear in this area are a bit lower than their cousins around
here. The layer of grass is thick and strangling all the other vegetation, as
all other growth needs to have an endless fight for their living space. The
endless plain seems to continue towards all directions from here.
name: A Lush Field

desc:
Much like the surrounding areas, grass and small weeds dominate this area. The
scenery remains a flat, slightly undulating grassland. The landscape rises to
the east, towards a slight elevation there. The grass is vital here, but it
still clearly suffers from the lack of humidity.
name: Arid Plains

desc:
The grass in the area is tall and dried out, and it looks like water has not
touched this area for a while. Whenever a calm breeze blows, the grass moves
like waves on an ocean on a windy day. Heat waves in the past have made the
ground cracked and gave the dirt a light brown colour.
name: Struggling Grass

desc:
A few dry standing trees here lost their lives and are slowly starting to decay.
The ground, like the trees, is very dry, cracked, and coloured by shades of
brown. Thin, short grass covers the area thoroughly, making it hard to see the
initial surface. Struggling vines hang down from dead branches of trees.
name: Dry Standing Trees

desc:
The dying grass and weeds colour this area brown and yellow. However, some
large, bare bushes seem to grow healthy here. A few partially rotten tree stumps
are still visible, dried by the arid climate as desertification has not yet
managed to strike its teeth to the area completely. Some low sand pits can be
detected around.
name: The Bushy Field

desc:
The stunted vegetation here is badly rotten, dry and overgrown by some lichen.
Shrubs, small trees, bushes - they all seem dead and far in the decomposition
process. A small, muddy and smelly stream flows from the south, only to
disappear in the damp, smelly soil. Further to the west, lush green fields open
as an opposite to this contaminated land.
name: Wasted Landscape

desc:
The water in the pond is muddy and has a light brown colour. A small stream
propels the liquid to the north. Lots of moss has gathered on different spots in
the pond. Some dark vapour rises from the water surface, emitting some heat to
the surrounding area. The slippery sides of the pond mean that extra care must
be taken here, lest one falls into the nauseating waters.
name: Murky Pond

desc:
The vegetation in this area has a healthy green colour, revealing a hint at
nutrients in the soil. Here and there, rowan trees sprout from old stumps which
have already decayed to a large degree. The grass is lush and tall, hiding most
details of the earthy surface. Open plains continue towards all directions
without any visible landmarks.
name: Open Plains

desc:
As the first thing, a huge termite hill meets the eye. Obviously, these termites
live from eating and destroying the decaying log wall to the west. From the
termite hill, a thin, black line runs on the ground, as those small creatures
pace around to get their meals. The ground around the termite hill is brown and
barren, while lush, green grass grows all around it.
name: A Termite Hill

desc:
A pile of old stones and logs lies here as a memorial to past days. Rotting logs
are still recognisable as a roof of an old building, now only waiting for time
to wear them out. The ground around the debris is bald and solid, formed by a
dry, thick layer of hardened soil. Only some brown vines climb up the pile.
name: Next to a Collapsed Building

desc:
At this spot, some unknown travellers have rested during their journeys, seeking
shelter below the old walls rising to the east. A large place for fire was made
next to the stone wall to the east, the stones still being blackened by ash of
long decayed campfires. A few rotten and mostly decayed furs lie on the ground.
name: A Discarded Camp

desc:
Some of the plants in this long abandoned garden are spread around, forming a
thick bed of flowers on the ground. Some parts of old stone fences can still be
seen around, mostly collapsed into piles of variously sized stones. Some rampant
growing old apple trees grow here, their roots buried deep under the ancient
stepping stones. Lush green fields open to all directions.
name: An Ancient Garden

desc:
Tall grass dominates this area, rustling and bending whenever a gust of wind
touches them. Some dry standing trees can be seen close to the west, while the
area to the east looks somewhat reshaped. Only some small bushes grow here, as
well as a few saplings of young rowan. The green field continues towards all
directions.
name: Rich Plains

desc:
A narrow path here leads towards an abandoned barn to the east and continues
towards the south, partially disappearing under the blooming growth. At some
places, only a few low, piled cairns indicate where the path has once run. Some
old rowan trees grow here, only accompanied by stunted growth of thorny bushes.
The landscape is fertile here, allowing the grass grow to tall and healthy.
name: Path through the Field

desc:
A wide and grass-covered area opens in front of an old building. A large, yet
abandoned barn stands there, made of medium-sized logs. The time and weather
have caused strong marks of decaying processes to appear on the walls. The door
of the barn swings back and forth along gusts of wind. Some weak remains of a
trail leave to the west from the door of the old barn.
name: Outside an Old Barn

desc:
The overgrown path here is approaching a building to the south. Low, partially
ruined stone fences rise on both sides of an old trail. Some wild bushes and
even a few larger trees have grown up on the path, indicating the eternity it
has been since the way was last used frequently. Lush, fertile grass fields open
to the east, while further to the south, some old ruins can be seen.
name: Faded Path

desc:
Endless plains open from behind the old building which blocks the way to the
north. Some weeds and wild cereals thrive here, stealing all nutrients from the
weak, infertile ground. A few low willow bushes and some young rowan trees grow
here, yet unable to absorb enough energy from the ground to gain any strength or
size.
name: Plains behind a Building

desc:
Tall, strong grass dominates this area. A few small herb bushes grow here,
adding some variation to the otherwise dull, even surface. Some faint animal
paths lead across the grass bed. Far to the west, there loom some old remains of
habitation as a lonely landmark on these flat fields. The plain area continues
towards the horizon, and even past it.
name: Open Plains

desc:
A few old stepping stones lie on the ground, mostly covered with soil, rotten
leaves and dust. The building itself consists of a stony basement and walls made
of huge, already decaying logs. A large termite hill can be seen close to the
south. Stunted grass grows around the area, allowing some low bushes to root on
the barren ground. A wide, open plain opens around the building.
name: Outside an Old Building

desc:
This barn used to be a shelter for animals. Now, it is a home for the termites,
cockroaches and spiders. Nobody has used this place for ages; the inner walls
are rotting, covered with lichen. Fusty, humid air stands still here, indicating
the decay. A few bones of a large animal lie on ground, mostly stuck in the
rotting wooden floor.
name: Abandoned Barn

desc:
The river Anduin flows here on a straight course from the north to the south.
The whirling current is strong and tries to drown everything it can catch. The
riverbanks are low and grassy, covered with a few small bushes growing on them.
Far to the west, the shape of a large forest can be seen shadowing the horizon
beyond a wide, grassy plain.
name: The Anduin

desc:
The Anduin is wide and shallow here, revealing a bright and clear sand bottom.
However, the current is very strong, dragging along everything it can catch.
Some branches quietly float southwards with rapid speed. The low, grassy
riverbank borders the riverbed to the west, while the riverbank to the east is
higher, making landing there impossible.
name: The Wide Spot in the Anduin

desc:
The river Anduin flows straight from the north to the south here. The riverbank
to the east is a low and gently sloping beach of fine sand. The low, sandy area
seems to continue further towards the east, still having trails of flowing water
there. The western bank is higher, consisting of soil, rocks and small bushes.
name: The Anduin

desc:
The river narrows into rapids, as its course sharply turns from the north to the
west. The riverbank to the south is high and steep, covered with large, bald
boulders which the force of water has washed clean of all soil. Some large
bushes fill the bank to the east, leaving only some openings to show a hint of
the vastness of the open plains behind them.
name: Bend in the Anduin

desc:
The great Anduin makes a wide turn from the east to the south here, being rather
calm but still strong by its current. The low riverbanks are covered with
man-sized rushes and a few young trees trying to sprout on the inner bank of the
bend. A grassy plain opens to the north and the west.
name: Bend in the Anduin

desc:
A sheer cliff wall stands here, blocking passage to the north. Many jagged rocks
lie on the ground here, as if they are acting as teeth for the bottom of the
cliff. High above, some patches of grass can be seen on the top of the hill. To
the east and the west it appears that the hill can be passed by walking around.
name: Rocky Bottom

desc:
This field is covered with dry weeds and tall grasses. The ground consists of
pebbles and does not appear very welcoming for any vegetation which might
attempt to grow here. The field is quite flat, with some rocks jutting out of
the ground in the northeast.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
A few plants of grain sprinkled in with thick growth of grass approach this
field from the north, but the ground is unforgiving to vegetation here. The soil
is too dense with stones and pebbles to allow grass to grow here, although some
persistent patches still live here and there. To the west there is an impressive
wall of brambles, intertwined in such a dense thicket that it will not allow any
travellers through at this point.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
Wild grain flourishes here in this fallow field, intermingled with grass. The
grain plants grow to several feet high, surpassing the tall blades of grass in
height. The seeds at the tops of the grain weigh some of plants down causing
them to bend and stoop.
name: Field of Grain

desc:
Brown grass covers the majority of this field. There are patches of green grass
growing in places but the overall hue of the field is of a golden-brown colour.
In the middle of the field some natural grain grows, rising above the brown
grass. The grain plants expand towards the east, dominating the horizon. There
is an impressive wall of bramble bushes to the west and south and there are also
some broken branches of bramble on the ground next to the bushes.
name: Patch of Grain

desc:
Thick brambles tangled together make up the huge vegetation wall to the north
and the east. The bramble bushes grow too thickly to the northeast for any
animals to pass through. The bushes begin to thin gradually from the east to the
west. In the west, some plains with low bramble bushes continue.
name: Dense Thicket

desc:
The riverside is steep and rocky, while the plains are covered with long grasses
and small bramble twigs, which become longer and thicker towards the east. The
sound of the rushing water of the Anduin comes from the west, as it continues
its southward flow.
name: On a Riverside

desc:
The bramble bushes grow wide apart from the south and begin to grow thicker
towards the north. Some patches of brush twigs are cut on the eastern side of
the bramble wall here. Overall the brush wall growing north to south looms large
here.
name: Bramble Bushes

desc:
Small pebbles cover the bank of the river, while grass covers the area further
from the river. On a smooth, sandy part of the bank, some thick wooden poles
have been erected; ropes, firmly attached around them, are visible. The
placement of the poles indicates a landing place for boats. The opposite shore,
covered with fallen trees, rises high above the river.
name: Pebbled Riverbank

desc:
Most of this riverbank is covered by a field of reeds, while the plains to the
east are covered with grass. An occasional bramble twig can be spotted between
the blades of grass, but further towards the east the brambles become more
dense, so dense that it is impossible to spot anything behind them.
name: On a Riverbank

desc:
There are flat plains grown over with grass covering this area. Some small
rounded rocks are scattered around thick, bushy sections of grass. Brambles,
bushes, and small thornbushes grow among the patches of grass surrounding the
rocks. Far to the southeast the contours of bushes are visible.
name: Flat Plains

desc:
These plains are covered with long grass. Here and there, dry brambles grow from
the ground, becoming very thick toward the south. The horizon to the south is
completely concealed by the wall of bramble, so thick that it cannot be seen
through, let alone travelled through.
name: Edge of the Bushes

desc:
These plains are flat in the northern part of this field but to the south the
ground gets higher and the vegetation becomes more dense; huge bramble bushes on
a hill can even be seen in the distance to the southwest. Apart from the
occasional animal tracks and droppings, the ground here does not have anything
more than grass.
name: Green Plains

desc:
Long grass covers the flat plains. At a few places some high bushes grow in
between the grass and there is even one dead tree. Small holes can be seen under
some of the bushes; typical for the entrance of rodent holes.
name: Flat Plains

desc:
The grass covered plains seem to go on forever to the east. Some small bushes
grow in between the grass. A barely visible animal track bends from the east to
the south. Far off, at the horizon to the south, dense and high bushes are
visible.
name: In the Plains

desc:
Typical swamp plants, such as reeds and irises grow at this submerged riverbank,
while the soft ground further away from the river is covered with long grass. In
some areas the grass seems to be trimmed down to a height just above the soil.
Some small excrement is visible around the trimmed areas, as if small animals
had grazed on the grass here.
name: On a Riverbank

desc:
The flat plains submerge into the calm river here. Various animal prints are
visible in the soft mud at the shore; this must be a frequently used drinking
place for the local wildlife. From the water front, an animal track leads inland
to the east.
name: On a Riverbank

desc:
The river can be seen flowing southward to the west of this riverbank. The
ground is rather high above the river in the north and gently slopes down as it
continues to the south. There are brown weeds that cover the ground and expand
to the east in a sea of dry plains.
name: Sloped Riverbank

desc:
This riverbank is surrounded by flowing water on two sides. The great river
Anduin flows westward then makes a turn south along the northwest edge of the
bank. The riverbank is fairly steep when approaching the surface of the river.
Short grass grows here continuing to the east and south.
name: Riverbank of the Anduin

desc:
Dark green grass covers the majority of this field, save a lone, dense tree
which stands proudly in the middle of the grass. The blades of grass are quite
short starting from the eastern side of the field and grow taller toward the
east. At some places the grass has been stamped down, a clear trail being
visible on the ground.
name: Green Field

desc:
Green grass covers this field which lies next to the great river Anduin to the
west. Where the meadow meets the river there is a short drop off into the river,
which does not cause any obstruction for a traveller wishing to swim. The meadow
continues in all other directions.
name: Green Meadow

desc:
Waist high grasses cover the ground in this meadow, which meets the Anduin river
to the north and the west. Reeds grow along the riverbank before meeting the
grasses of the meadow on this side of the river. The meadow continues to the
east and south.
name: Green Meadow

desc:
This meadow grows waist high grasses and a few weeds interspersed throughout.
The ground is a little soggy, causing fertile soil for a lush, green look to the
grass growing in the field here. The ground gradually slopes upward to the
south.
name: Green Meadow

desc:
Short grass and irises cover this low-lying riverbank. The ground is soggy but
solid enough to walk on without slowing one down. Reeds border the river to the
west. The ground begins to gradually slope upward to the east and south.
name: Low Riverbank

desc:
The field slopes down from the east, covered with a spattering of flowers and
grasses. The grass is quite healthy here in this low green field. On the western
horizon the ground continues to lose elevation. To the south the green fields
and assorted flora continue.
name: Green Field

desc:
The ground seems to slope downwards toward the west here, creating a natural
protection against the wind in this otherwise flat landscape. The local flora
has seized control of this protected field; flowers and other plants are growing
in abundance. The plains south of here seem rather barren with the exception of
some short bushes.
name: Green Plains

desc:
While the riverbank towards the northeast is steep and grassy, the shore of this
area sits a bit lower. The rich assortment of rocks on this riverbank add a
colourful grey texture compared to the rich blue of the Anduin river. They have
been smoothed and polished from years of the river rushing over them as it makes
its way southwest.
name: A Rocky Shore

desc:
Bushes and small trees grow here, but the most notable landmark would be an old
cottage. Vines cover it from top to bottom, as it has been left by caring hands
here for quite some time, and nature has taken it over. Just west of it lies a
huge millstone.
name: Ingrown Bower

desc:
Four columns mark there was once a house here, though the remnants have been
burned and blackened. Much of the debris is covered with a green moss, while
others are slowly sinking into the soil. Absolutely nothing stands to give
shelter to anyone travelling through here.
name: Burned Ruins

desc:
Tufts of grass and small rocks dot this extending wasteland, giving it a
desolate look. The caked soil is unforgiving to the grass that has sprouted up,
relentlessly forcing it to struggle for its life among the rocks.
name: Grassy Wasteland

desc:
The riverbank is covered in brush, while the fields around it are green with
grasses. The brush and soil have been trampled on several places, indicating
that animals have made the riverbank and its surrounding fields their home.
name: Brush-littered Riverbank

desc:
Many small trees grow on the plains here, among a few stumps that dwindle in
number as they span deeper into the landscape. While the top of the trees makes
a somewhat healthy impression that cannot be said about the lower parts,
especially near the roots. The soil is somewhat soft and smells slightly of mud,
the grass and bushes look bleak and dying while patches of fungi thrieve
everywhere. In many places the fungi have started to grow between the trees'
roots and have started to climb even higher.
name: Woody Plains with Rotting Vegetation

desc:
The landscape of this area has been harshly ravaged by some woodworkers. The
mottled landscape is littered with stumps of varying heights; who ever chopped
this field bare did so very hastily, and without any care to its surroundings.
The Great River flows directly to the west, with a huge mess of logs stuck in
the brush on the riverbank.
name: Field of Stumps next to the Anduin

desc:
A huge wooden dock, built from heavy logs, covers the terrain like a massive
floor. The first pilings of the dock have crumbled so that the dock floor is
tilted and slowly descends into the river, creating an unstable stair into the
water. However, the dock can still be used as a landing area for small boats.
What seems to be a trail begins here, and continues on to the east.
name: Ruined Log Dock

desc:
The well worn trail bends here, leading to the west and south. Old wagon tracks
are occasionally visible on the edges of the trail. It appears to not have been
in active use in recent years, with patches of tall grass covering the trail in
many areas.
name: Bend in an Old Trail

desc:
A line of trees grows along the northern border of a trail coming from the river
and heading southeast. The trail cuts the plain deeply. Descending from here to
the road is impossible due to the steepness of the escarpment, while travelling
to the east along a gentle slope will lead to the trail.
name: Line of Trees

desc:
The ground raises towards a small hill to the south. The slope is covered by
grass and small plants, and some occasional gullies make the ground uneven and
somewhat hard to travel. The top of the hill is covered by a patch of trees.
Further to the south, a sort of road comes from the west and, turning around a
steep rise, vanishes to the southeast. The plain continues to the north and
east.
name: Sloping Meadow

desc:
The ground is completely covered by high grass. Some stems are two feet high
with little panicles to the top, some other are even higher with a flower-head
made of densely packed spikelets. To the west some trees dot the plain. To the
north the vegetation becomes thick and hard to travel.
name: High Grass

desc:
Rocks jut out from all directions along the walls here. This cave seems to have
been hollowed out ages ago from some water source. Now it can be used as a
hiding place for any travellers needing shelter. The cave entrance is quite wide
but begins to narrow gradually as it burrows its way to the west.
name: Cave Entrance

desc:
This hollowed out cave comes to its end here as it becomes too narrow and
finally disappears into a stone wall on the west. There are some ancient crates
lying about in disarray here; perhaps some supplies for some ancient travellers,
using the cave as a shelter.
name: Back of the Cave

desc:
Some patches of dry, thirsty grass grow here and there in this sloping field.
The ground begins to slope upward to the east, as it approaches a hill. Many
pebbles cover the ground here causing a crunching sound underfoot.
name: Dry, Sloping Plains

desc:
The sloping ground gives way to a stony peak here. There are some large boulders
on this hill, and the ground is covered with small stones. Patches of grass grow
among the boulders, and a few small bushes grow here and there as well. The
rocky hill is smooth in its descent on all sides, except to the south, which
gives way to a sheer cliff. The cliff bottom below to the south can be easily
viewed from here but tall bushes and boulders block the view to any other
direction.
name: Rocky Hillock

desc:
The field is filled with long, green blades of grass. Some of them being so
light, that their ends curl over, dipping back towards the earth. The ground
begins to slope upward from the east to the west. A hill can be seen climbing in
the horizon to the west. Some large boulders stick out of the ground here and
there.
name: Sloping Field

desc:
This field has healthy, green grass growing at waist level, but the most
striking feature of the field is the amount of wildflowers growing here. There
are irises growing in the north, but as the field unfolds toward the south,
wildflowers of all types emerge in the field, peaking their petals over the top
of the grass.
name: Field of Wildflowers

desc:
This is a vast plain of waist high grass and wild flowers, dominated by plants
with forked leaves and white composite flowers. To the north the ground starts
descending and the bushes of bramble, which are rare here, start growing denser.
Nests in the branches of larger and older bushes and frequent cries of birds
indicate active wild life.
name: Field Covered in White

desc:
Dark-green hemlock plants with finely divided leaves and strong, spotted stems
prosper and dominate the vegetation. Their large umbels consisting of numerous
small, white flowers attract hordes of insects and butterflies, which fill the
air with the sounds of buzzing. Fields clothed in white continue in the west,
whereas in the north, where the grass gets darker, some small hills dot the
landscape.
name: Field Covered in White

desc:
An enormous tree towers above the fields here, having withstood many years of
raging storm, but now finally faltering due to old age. Its lower branches are
dry and dead, and the trunk is hollow. Yet the leaves on upper branches are
fresh and green, showing that the tree has not entirely given up. Bees fly
continuously in and out of the huge, hollow trunk, and loud buzzing fills the
air, apparently coming from a little opening in the trunk. A muddy-looking,
bubbling swamp opens up to the east, blocking the travelling to that direction.
name: At a Hollow Tree

desc:
The humming and buzzing sound becomes deafening inside the trunk, as the bees do
not like intruders. No wood is actually visible, because all sides of the trunk
are covered with waxy honeycombs and are swarming with drones. Despite the
immense size of the tree, there is not much room in this chamber, making it hard
to fight the drones guarding their nest. A small opening in the trunk leads to
the plains outside of the tree.
name: Inside a Trunk

desc:
Many colourful wild flowers flourish in this dense, almost impassable thicket.
During summer time, when all the flowers bloom and fill the dense brush with
sweet fragrance of pollen, numerous bees are attracted here. Faint buzzing and
humming sounds fill the air when the bees work hard to collect nectar, and when
they fly home, they are packed heavy with their load.
name: Flowery Thicket

desc:
The northern portion of this field is comprised of knee-high grass. There are a
few weeds here and there in the northern half. The southern part of this field
consists of a mass of thorny bushes, tangled together in a never ending
wrestling match. Brambles grow on the eastern side of the brush, mixing in with
the thornbushes. The ground towards the west begins to become less populated
with vegetation and some rocks are revealed on the bare ground.
name: In the Thornbushes

desc:
Thick brambles intertwined with razor sharp thornbushes and thick raspberry
bushes comprises this almost impenetrable field of brush. Berries can be seen
within the tangle of brush but for the most part all that is visible are dried
stems with sharp thorns attached.
name: Thorny Brambles

desc:
The level ground is covered with a thicket of wild raspberry reaching higher
than a man's waist. Tangled thorny canes which are frequently impenetrable force
travellers to search for easier routes. Barbed plants scratch the body and a
layer of dried stems below the feet makes crunching sounds with each step. Some
of the still standing stems are dry and dead, whereas others carry leaves and
have remains of little berry clusters.
name: Thick Raspberry Bushes

desc:
Patches of wild raspberries with four feet high canes are interspersed with
stinging nettles, forming a thicket which stings and tears the exposed skin of
any travellers. Old stems, thorny vines and branches form a mass which is hard
to penetrate. The earth beneath the plants is hard and dry, waiting for long-
needed water.
name: Thick Raspberry Bushes

desc:
Various wild flowers and flowering weeds liven up this grassy slope with
colourful tints and hues. During summer time the air is filled with humming and
buzzing sounds of bees and other small insects, whereas in winter time the
tracks of small animals indicate active wildlife.
name: Flowering Slope

desc:
The meadow is covered by lush grass dotted by red and white flowers stretching
towards the sun above the grass. A small calm stream with dark cold water
moistens the vegetation, also attracting numerous small insects buzzing above
the water. Animal tracks all around the muddy banks and in shallow water
indicate that wild animals use this spot for drinking.
name: At the Animal Drinking Site

desc:
The land is covered with tall grasses interspersed with flowering plants, which
are growing in thick bushes around the slowly bubbling brook. Huge splotches of
bracken and bramble coloured in different tones of green give the plain a
mottled look. The ground gets harder in the south and east, where the wetland
plants are replaced with more common grass.
name: Wet Plain

desc:
The ground dips here, causing water to collect and saturate the soil. Swamp
plants thrive in this area. There are flourishing swamp irises growing all about
here. Healthy grass surrounds the depression here in all directions except the
east.
name: Wet Plain

desc:
Long blades of grass, along with some weeds, fill this field. Some of the grass
blades have a glossy, shiny appearance. The weeds that grow here bend over at
the top. To the west the ground begins to slope upward. There are some large
stones lying around.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
The ground begins to gain elevation here, starting from the north. The slope in
the landscape gives way to a hill in the south. The hill itself is quite bare of
vegetation. There are some large stones sticking out of the ground and some
patches of grass here and there. In the west some light brush can be seen. Tall
grass can be seen to the east.
name: Sloped Fields

desc:
Thick bushes grow in the middle of this field, laced together with a scraggly
thornbush. The leaves of the bushes grow outward, as if trying to escape the
grasp of the thornbush, reaching for sunlight. To the southeast the ground
begins to gain elevation and the soil is less fertile, with a sprinkling of
pebbles. A decrepit farmhouse can be seen to the west.
name: Bushy Field

desc:
Lying in the middle of the field is the remains of an ancient farmhouse. Dust
covers everything in this small farm house, which appears to have been abandoned
long ago. A broken chair sits on its side next to an old table. There is debris
lying about within the ruined frames of the farmhouse but nothing of value. The
farmhouse seems to have given shelter to one or two occupants who once tilled
the fields to the north, but no one has lived here for many years.
name: Ruined Farmhouse

desc:
Brown coloured weeds and numerous jagged boulders cover the slopes of this small
hill; creating small ravines, grooves and deceptive deadends all around the
rough ground. Along the eastern slope, the remnants of an ancient fence are
running from north to south, but so decrepit is the fence that it hardly poses
any obstacle to travelling. Beyond the fence a neglected farmhouse can be seen.
Numerous bramble bushes are growing along the western edge of the hill, making
it impossible to pass through. The vegetation begins to turn greener further to
the north.
name: Hill of Jagged Rocks

desc:
The former habitants of this long ago abandoned farm must have used this cellar
as a place for storage, as there are left over boxes and crates lying about,
collecting dust. There are a few broken barrels and some dusty, broken glass,
leftover from a broken bottle of wine lying on the ground. Dust covers
everything in this ancient cellar, which has not been used for ages.
name: Old Cellar

desc:
The ground here looks as if it was cultivated in ages before. There is some
debris lying about in the field, nothing discernible. The land appears to have
once held a healthy crop, but has long since been abandoned. To the north some
bushes conceal the horizon, on the eastern and western sides of the farm the
ground is wet and fertile.
name: Abandoned Farmland

desc:
Soggy ground to the east gives way to a drier, more solid soil in this field.
Lush, tall grass blades grow unhindered here by weeds or other vegetation,
thriving in the sunlight. A short, ancient fence stands here, running in a north
to south direction. The fence is ancient and decrepit, and hardly gives an
obstacle.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
These plains are overgrown with dark green reeds and tall cattails. There is
also a short layer of lush, healthy green coloured grass growing on the ground.
The grass on the ground seems very delicate though, as if even a footstep would
disturb the ground and uproot the grass. A small brook, originating from the
north flows southbound, then makes a turn towards the east here.
name: Bend in the Brook

desc:
The brook flows gently to the east as it tumbles over rocks. There are reeds
growing on either side of the brook and further outward from the brook grows
healthy grass. There are clear tracks here, showing that this is a common
drinking spot for many of the small animals that live in the surrounding plains.
name: Wet Plain beside the Brook

desc:
A dark, shallow brook flows calmly from the west to the south through this
entangled, mould-smelling area. The numerous roots, bushes and high grass thrive
around the brook, accompanied by tall patches of reeds and cattails growing both
in the water and in depressions near the brook. The dense shrubbery have
eventually taken over almost the whole area, only to the east the terrain is
clearer, as it rises below the bushes towards a rugged ridge.
name: Wet Thicket around the Brook

desc:
The ground sinks abruptly to the north, providing a clear and unblocked view to
the vast plains that extend everywhere to the north. The top of this ridge is
stony and bare, save a huge, lone fir tree which has rooted itself firmly to the
ground, bearing the sun, wind and rains without any signs of weakening: Its
branches spread widely and trunk is covered in grey lichens.
name: A Lone Tree on the Ridge

desc:
The plains expand in every direction here. The brown weeds that populate the
western area of the plains intermingle with lush, green, blades of grass. The
occurrence of green in the plains increases gradually to the east.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Nothing more than grass covers the ground. Small holes are visible in the soil.
Tracks of a fox are visible around one of the excavated holes, along with the
remaining earth work of a worm. Most of the grassy soil is trampled by animals.
A small animal track running east-west is visible in the grass.
name: In the Plains

desc:
Tall, healthy grasses populate this area. There are a few brown-tipped blades of
grass on the western side, but the grass seems to thrive on fertile soil to the
east. Some areas on the eastern side become very soft, even swampy. Thick,
dark-green bushes grows to the north.
name: Lush Grasslands

desc:
The dense bushes are almost impenetrable here; they grow all about in this area.
The sound of birds, safe from enemies, can be heard from the surrounding bramble
bushes. The bramble bushes create an impassable wall of intertwined branches to
the north and towards the east.
name: Dense Bramble Bushes

desc:
Small, yellow flowers cover this fertile field in large clusters. The hardy
plants seem to have no trouble growing where other flowers would have a hard
time surviving. To the north and east, the open field ends abruptly to a tall
wall of bushes, and the landscape seems to rise a bit there. Wide grasslands,
with flowers dispersing gradually, stretch to the west.
name: Flower-covered Field

desc:
The lush grassland is covered by flowers and small bushes. Small animals seem to
forage around here, as several piles of animal droppings and half-eaten berries
are lying on the ground. There is also an entrance to what appears to be a
rather large foxhole. To the north, the amount of bushes increases, and the
terrain transforms into a thick, continuous mass of brush.
name: In a Grassland

desc:
A large pack of foxes appears to have lived in this hole at some point in time
but now it is empty. There are some rotting bones in the corner, perhaps left
over from the foxes and whatever must have eaten them. There are slithering
tracks covering the ground in here, appearing to be from a snake.
name: An Empty Fox Hole

desc:
The ground does not have the level of vegetation that can be seen in all other
directions here. The lack of grass allows all of the pebbles and stones to be
exposed on the ground. The grass is lush and green from the north, until it hits
the sparse ground here. To the east brown weeds and green grasses continue
despite the barren ground here.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
The plains here are quite high. The grasses seem moderately thick here, but
begin to thin out toward the east. Towards the south some small animal holes are
visible. To the west in the distance a river is visible. Dark green vegetation
can be seen in the north.
name: High Plains

desc:
Waist high, green grass blades growing with a lush glossy texture gradually thin
out until they meet dry ground in the southern half of this field. Patches of
grass and weeds continue on the sparse soil but rocks and stones cover the
majority of the ground. To the east, an old gate stands in a narrow opening
between dense bushes.
name: Edge of a Green Field

desc:
An old, abandoned-looking gate stands between two impassable thickets of
vegetation. Lush growth embraces the gate from the both sides, long hay grows
all around it, and the surface of its sturdy, wooden bars has been occupied by
light-green moss. Nevertheless, the gate looks stout and strong.
name: An Old, Weathered Gate

desc:
A rugged clearing opens up inside the thicket. In the middle of this brushy area
lies the remains of a huge, old tree, broken and fallen to the ground. Branches
are withering away from age and lie scattered about, soon to be reclaimed by the
nature. The stump of the tree, as well as thick roots, still stand firmly
planted in the ground. Around the glade, a continuous, dense thicket extends to
all directions.
name: Fallen Tree

desc:
This fertile grove is covered with grass and some flowers, along with weeds of
many types. There are long, slender grass-type weeds and squat, sprawling weeds
low to the ground. Some of the weeds have needle sharp stickers, warning any
travellers from trampling them. Countless willow trees are surrounding the
grove, seemingly enjoying the moist soil and thriving in growth.
name: Grove of Willows

desc:
This bushy area is dominated by a large pool of cold water. Originating from a
small tunnel beneath a big boulder, the water trickles forth creating a natural
waterhole. In the outskirts of the water, reed-like grass grows first in
clusters as high as a man, creating excellent cover for smaller animals to nest
in, and then transforms into a dense, unfathomable shrubbery.
name: Placid Pool of Water

desc:
Water drips from all around in this wet, humid pit. There are streams of water
running along the ceiling of the cavern all around. There is a light underground
stream running along the bottom of the pit, draining the water that collects
from all around.
name: Humid Pit

desc:
Roots intertwine with the hard earthen walls inside this hollowed out trunk. The
smell of rotting wood and vegetation intermingled with the musty smell of earth
fills the humid, hollow cavern. Evidence of wood-eating insects shows on the
surface areas of the trunk.
name: Inside a Trunk

desc:
Wide bushes, roots, and dense undergrowth have intertwined together, forming an
impassable thicket that surrounds this small sanctuary. The ground is dominated
by unorganized patches of dark green bushes, although they are less dense to the
south where grass takes over. The soil looks rather fertile; small patches of
yellow flowers spring here and there between the bushes.
name: In the Shade of a Brushwood

desc:
These barren fields hold little support for anything except low grass. The soil
here seems to be sandy and dry. To the northeast, the view is partially obscured
by high stands of what looks to be reeds. Green plains stretch out to the east
and west, while to the south, giant bushes block the way forward.
name: Large Field

desc:
Light green grass covers this field. There are a few wild flowers growing in the
northern part of this field but for the most part it is only grass. Being that
the light green grass is the dominant feature here, the field has an overall
single colour of green to it.
name: Green Plains

desc:
Grass is growing in the northern and the western portions of this field, while a
dense wall of dark-green vegetation dominates the area to the south and east.
Weeds of many types are growing here, thriving in the fertile soil and plentiful
sunlight.
name: Field of Weeds

desc:
This field is covered with meadow plants of many types. There are wheat grasses
mixed with regular grass, with waist high weeds. Tall dandelions are also
growing in the field. This ground is somewhat higher than the fields in all
other directions.
name: Green Field

desc:
Standing on a large field of green grass, there are no topical marks whatsoever.
The ground is dotted with occasional clusters of taller grass. To the south and
west the field stretches out seemingly forever. To the north the view is
obstructed by taller, reed-like grasses. The sound of open water can be heard
from the east.
name: Large Field

desc:
Several small rocks protrude out of the ground in this bleak, barren field where
little else but grass and low bushes grow, bending and hugging the land under
the pressure of the cold, harsh wind. High grass and untamed, continuous thicket
of willows and other small trees border this area to the west and south. The
ground appears more fertile to the north and east.
name: Bleak Field

desc:
Stretching all across the land like a blanket, a sea of grass covers the area
here. The grass continues to the east where it is met by brush which gradually
takes over the horizon. A deep and rather sweet smell rises from the ground. A
few bumblebees hover over some wildflowers nearby.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
High, reed-like clusters of dark green grass cover the land, giving protection
to various rodents that inhabit this area from the frequent winds. Moist and
soft ground exhibits evidence of some rat prints, it seems larger animals also
visit these fields. Grasslands dominate the landscape in every direction but
west, where a solid wall of brushwood blocks the way.
name: Field of Tall Grass

desc:
The soil lying under these grasslands is very moist and fertile. Water trickles
in a small brook from the west, having somehow found its way through the
impassable-looking thicket that stands there. Animal tracks can be seen on the
ground and this place looks well-travelled. The brook continues its way to the
south, bending around several large stones, before disappearing into a reed-
filled swamp. The sound of running water fills the air.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
Occasional bushes that cover this otherwise grass-dominated landscape slowly
wave in the wind. The sweet and gentle smell of flowers fills the air. Majestic
plains continue to the west, yet dark green brush begins to take over the
grasslands to the east. A sinister-looking bog opens up to the south, blocking
all travelling there.
name: Light Plains

desc:
Small evergreen bushes spot the landscape and slowly flow into a dense thicket
to the north. Strangely, hardly any grass covers the sandy yellow coloured earth
and hoof prints mark the ground. Grasslands go on to the west farther than eyes
can see. To the south, the terrain transforms into a deceptive swamp.
name: Bushy Lands

desc:
The grass around here is low and burnt yellow by the sun, lacking the necessary
moisture. At places where the dry soil is exposed, small cracks of contraction
are visible. A sun-bleached skeleton of some large animal lies on the ground,
with most of its bones intact. Grass has already started to grow through the
rib-bones, soon hiding the skeleton from view.
name: Skeleton in the Grass

desc:
Among the surrounding grassland rises a small clump of tall pines consisting of
no more than ten trees. The ground underneath is layered with old needles,
pieces of dry branches, and thin slices of brown bark, which together form a
natural carpet. In the centre of the clump, stacked against a tall pine, resides
a huge ant-hill. The hill is over five feet high, consisting mostly of dry brown
needles, earth, and some sand.
name: An Ant Hill under the Pine Trees

desc:
The huge expanse of endless green grass continues in every direction except
north, where it is broken by the silhouettes of small jagged hills. Long grass
growing all around is laying near the ground, probably the result of strong
winds blowing across the plain. Winds also bring sweet scents of flowering
plants mixed with the smell of fresh grass and moist earth.
name: Fragrant Meadow

desc:
Many bright, fragrant wildflowers push up from the meadow covered in two-feet
high grass, livening it up. Even though the grass is dark green and looks strong
and healthy, indicating fertile soil, no trees nor any plants higher than a man
grow here. Long honeysuckle branches hanging over the grass give the meadow a
distinct sweet smell, which also attracts numerous bees during summer months.
name: Fragrant Meadow

desc:
Even though no flowing water is visible, the soil here is moist and squishy.
Saturated with water throughout most of the year, the meadow has a large variety
of different grasses and herbs which prosper in these conditions. The meadow is
the home and breeding ground for numerous different birds, who have easy access
to food and good nesting places.
name: Wet Meadow

desc:
This huge meadow of lush green grass would make good farmland, as the soil is
fertile and moist. Some large animals must have grazed here, judging by the
grass which at places lies chewed down and trampled. To the north and east the
meadows continue, whereas to the south the ground rises abruptly to the top of a
rugged hillock.
name: Lush Meadow

desc:
Eventhough the tall grass growing here is bent down by frequent winds rushing
along the steep ridge to the south, the grass is still particulary thick and
healthy. A small pond has formed to the plains, collecting water from the areas
above, and providing enough watersupply to all fauna and flora around here.
Bubbling sounds of water are mixed with quiet splashes, which are probably made
by small fish rocketing out of the pond.
name: Plains beside a Steep Ridge

desc:
The ground is covered with lush flowering grass and lowers slightly to the west
and south, where the vegetation is dominated by wetland plants, and terrain
transforms gradually to a dangerous-looking bog, too deceptive to be entered
safely. Some stunted rowan trees growing on higher places are the only tall
objects here, forming harder islets in the muddy and soft ground. Evergreen ivy
plants climb on the trees, almost hiding their barks under thick layer of leaves
and stems.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
The rolling moorland with yellow, sparse grass slightly slopes downwards to the
east, dotted with numerous bushes and shrubs, but no trees grow in this barren
soil. Interspersed with the coarse grass are tufts of purple heather, which
colour the otherwise bleak land. Stronger winds pick up gritty dust and throw it
in the air, making travelling unpleasant.
name: Rolling Moors

desc:
The rolling moors of lush, green grass have traces of ancient habitation; some
irregular mounds with simple stone monuments are visible amidts the vegetation.
A straight line of old and majestic ash trees crosses the moors. Elsewhere, the
landscape is dotted with ancient oak trees marking old vantage points. Towards
the north a steep side of knoll blocks the path. There seem to be some deep
cracks a bit higher up in the rockface, large enough for a man to hide in. The
moor below that steep rockface is very dark, rotten, and exudes a foul stench.
name: Rolling Moors next to a Steep Hillside

desc:
A sandy slope leads into a dry valley in the east which seems to have been
formed by flowing water. Various colours of limestone are revealed on the rough
walls at either side of the riverbed, which are high and impossible to climb.
The dry bottom is covered with pebbles of different sizes interspersed with low
grass. Small, crippled trees cling to the crumbling limestone, struggling to
survive on the almost vertical walls of stone.
name: Mouth of an Old Riverbed

desc:
The old, dry riverbed cutting through the ground in an east-west direction
splits the surrounding plains into two. Thorny bushes growing on the southern
side are replaced with smooth grassland in the north. Water that eroded through
the layers of limestone is now gone, leaving the gully dry. Even though no green
plants have rooted on the steep sides, a layer of low grass is growing on the
bottom.
name: An Old Riverbed

desc:
The ancient, dry riverbed makes a turn here, going from west to south. Rocky
walls rise high on either side, giving no handholds for climbing up. The bottom,
which was once filled with clear water, is now just a sandy trail, marked with
rounded pebbles and occasional small shrubs. Faint trails of snakes in the sand
and cries of birds high up are the only indications of wildlife in this lone
place.
name: Turn in an Old Riverbed

desc:
The ancient riverbed cutting through the soft rock in the north suddenly ends
here, leaving only looming walls of rock on every side. The water, which once
poured from an underwater cave, is long gone, and huge boulders from the rocky
walls have blocked the way down completely. The rocks and small pebbles are
round and smooth, worn by the water rushing by in the distant past.
name: Dead End of an Old Riverbed

desc:
The landscape here consists mostly of rolling grasslands which curve upwards and
finally level in the south. Some stunted firs peep out from amid the thick green
foliage growing on the slope, struggling to survive. The alternating vegetation
consists mainly of tough weeds and grasses which are replaced with thorny bushes
in the east.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
Rolling grasslands suddenly end with a thorny thicket in the east. The dense
brush growing in a straight line is twice as tall as a grown man, making passage
in this direction hard. Thorns and sharp, dry branches hinder movement,
scratching travellers. Foliage has moved high up towards the sun, leaving lower
branches without leaves. A slightly musty smell surrounds the damp darkness in
the heart of the brush.
name: Thorny Thicket

desc:
The bushes that flourish to the south have grown to the very edge of this valley
and some of the smaller plants even cling to the face of the cliff in an attempt
to gather light. The plants look as if they may have been planted in times past
to act as a wind barrier, but have now grown high and dense, making passage
impossible to the east and south, whereas the passage to the north is blocked by
the vertical walls of the valley.
name: Brush Overlooking a Valley

desc:
The damp, marshy ground carries many marks of animal feet both upon the short
grass and amid the clusters of tough weeds covering the ground. Occasionally
small rivulets wind through the grass, feeding little green-scummed pools. To
the west the land drops slightly and grasses give way to sedges and reeds.
name: East of a Marsh

desc:
The plains are split by a small valley, which runs from north to south. Even
though the valley is not deep and only a small stream runs along its bottom, it
looks like a scar in the earth, separating the vegetation. The western side of
the valley is bordered by reeds and ferns growing on wet and muddy ground, while
grasses and brush cover the drier ground in the east.
name: Small Valley

desc:
The valley runs in a straight line from north to south, like being cut with an
enormous knife into the earth. It continues towards the horizon in the north,
but ends in the south where the small stream on the bottom suddenly dissappears
under rocks. Tones of brown dominate in the west where the plains are wet and
muddy, while green-grey coloured grasses grow east of the crevice where the
ground is more sandy and drier.
name: Small Valley

desc:
The fields are clothed thickly with heather, with their tiny flowers giving
purple tint to the plains even during the wintertime. Little patches of low
grass struggle their last match, but the heather has won. It is virtually
everywhere, even pushing up from the barren soil beneath the rocks in the north,
where the plains suddenly stop. In the west, however, the heather is gradually
replaced with wetland plants.
name: Heathery Plains

desc:
The ground here is quite wet, but the vegetation looks dull and small. Besides
the clusters of low heather and moss only a few crippled alders are scattered
around. Barks of the trees are shaded in brown and black tones, and only a few
of their branches have leaves. Most of the plants are growing atop of their dead
relatives, forming soft springy layers on the ground. Farther to the west, the
plain ends abruptly to a continuous wall of brush.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
A small pond hides in a thicket of willow trees. Its cold water is clear and
fresh, yet no plants grow along its sandy bottom. The shores are flanked with
heather, which covers the surrounding plains with uniform carpet. The quietness
of the pond is occasionally disturbed by the noises of small animals quickly
flitting away and hiding among the heather.
name: A Pond in Heathery Plains

desc:
The plains stretch out for miles in every direction, with only a very few
changes in the landscape. A carpet of heather interspersed with low-growing
shrubs covers the ground, concealing shallow fox holes and pitfalls. Occasional
granite boulders, polished by the rain and wind, are half-hidden among taller
shrubs, which also give shelter to small animals.
name: Heathery Plains

desc:
The hard ground is overgrown with thorny bushes and tough weeds, all coloured in
grey-green to brown tones. A few patches of brown grass struggle in the shadows
of smaller bushes, but the thorny weeds are taking up all the nourishment from
the soil, leaving nothing for smaller plants. To the east the hedges grow denser
and denser until they finally block the way, but it might be possible to clear a
path through the tangle in the west.
name: Thorny Brushwood

desc:
The small passage in between the thick thorns is easy to follow, but thriving
bushes in the east and west threaten to close the way soon. Bigger bushes have
reached the size of trees, and in east and west their branches are interwoven
with each other, making passage impossible. The damp air is filled with the
smell of rotting wood and the only sound is the rustling of the wind in the
thorns.
name: Natural Passageway

desc:
A narrow tunnel leads steeply down into a small, earthy cave. Tiny bones lie
scattered on the ground, and the penetrating smell of animal excrement emenating
from the darkest corner is mixed with the smell of moist earth. Fresh clawprints
are clearly visible on the sandy ground, leading in both directions.
name: Earthy Cavern

desc:
Thorny bushes dominate the vegetation, spreading over the fields. Most other
plants have given up the fight for space and only a few patches of low brown
grass struggle to survive. Concealed beneath the thick vegetation, a few
weathered boulders are half-hidden under a soft layer of moss. To the west the
density of bushes increases and their branches are interwoven with each other,
making any movement through the brush difficult, whereas it might be possible to
open a way through the hedge in the east.
name: Thornbushes

desc:
The crops of a long-gone farmer are still growing, but the once well-tended
fields are now partially overgrown with wild weeds and grasses. In the west the
thorny hedge that once protected the fields from winds sweeping across the
plains has grown high, making passage in this direction seemingly impossible.
Low stone walls surrounding the fields in other sides have decayed with age and
are covered with a layer of moss.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
A small village that once stood here has long been abandoned. Nature has started
claiming back the land: low walls are all that remain of formerly taller
buildings, barely standing out amid the brush that flourishes everywhere.
Streets connecting the ruins are covered with creeping grass interspersed with
occasional loose stones peeking out from under the overgrowth.
name: Ruins of an Old Village

desc:
Most of the orchard has been cut down by axes, with only straight lines of
stumps remaining. In the southern part some of the old and gnarled apple trees
are still standing, but their trunks are cracked from bearing too much fruit.
Crippled pines and other conifers have started to replace the fruit trees at the
eastern side, and towards the dark line of brush looming to the west the trees
also fade, giving way to woody, dark bushes.
name: Old Orchard

desc:
The small valley is occupied and surrounded by thick, impenetrable bushes.
Shrubs of blackberries grow around the thickets, holding the soil and small
pebbles in place with their roots, but elsewhere in the valley the lower
vegetation has been suffocated by the tall, twelve feet high thickets. During
the day time, the light passing through the high plants makes shadows dance on
the ground as the leaves move in the wind.
name: Brush Overlooking a Valley

desc:
Amid the plains of green grass is a clump of sparse beech trees, looking old and
ageless. The ground around the trees is covered with a thick carpet of
bluebells, so dense that they leave almost no room for any other plants. In the
spring, when the flowers bloom, bees will come here to collect their first
nectar. Meandering paths around the trees and small droppings on the ground
indicate that the area is populated with little animals.
name: Sea of Bluebells

desc:
The grass on the fields around the village is lush and green, concealing rich
soil underneath. Even though the crops are mostly replaced with wild weeds, they
sometimes still push up through the lower grass. Around the long-abandoned ruins
in the west runs a low stone wall that harsh sun and pouring rain have worn even
lower. A very faint footpath leads from the ruins to the south, towards a larger
trail.
name: East of a Village

desc:
A narrow, barely visible trail winds across the plains, twisting and turning to
find a path among thornbushes and high grass. The trail is rarely used, but it
still offers a less tiring route than wading through the surrounding vegetation.
On rare occasions small items like a broken arrowhead can be found among the
strip of grass fringing the trail.
name: Winding Trail in the Plains

desc:
Huge cattails have replaced all other plants in this swampy area, reaching out
of low-looking water and thick, black mud. At the end of cattails' strong stems
are brown cigar-shaped catkins, which are grouped with tall sword-like leaves.
The swamp continues to the north and west, while the other directions seem to
lead to drier places. The air is filled with the sounds of small insects and
chorus of frogs.
name: Dense Cattails in a Swamp

desc:
A meadow full of thick, rusting grass has turned into a wet, foul bog a while
ago. Water has collected in lower parts of the meadow, and swampholes seem to be
somewhat of a danger below the stinking, decaying surface of old vegetation. One
of these patches seems particularly large, yet also even more foul than the
others.
name: A Foul-smelling Swamp

desc:
The ground is covered with a thick layer of moss and lichen forming a
deceivingly stable-looking green and brown carpet. Underneath the layer,
however, is concealed soft mud, ready to suck in the legs of uncautious
travellers. Here and there grow clusters of small plants, looking sick and
barely alive. The air is filled with the noises of myriads of different small
insects, and from time to time bubbling sounds can be heard from the ground.
name: Muddy Ground

desc:
The low, green grass that covers the ground is lush and soft, with occasional
patches of red and white clover giving it a mottled look. The soil underneath is
black and fertile, as visible from molehills, which frequently rise above
underground burrows. In the west the ground gradually slopes up, with some small
hillocks looking over the surroundings, whereas in all other directions the
plains continue.
name: Low Grass

desc:
Many small, stony hillocks are grouped together in the plains, looking
remarkably similar. Among the moss-covered rocks on the sides of some hillocks
grow small blueberry shrubs, whereas the others are covered with dried stems of
wild raspberry. Dried droppings on the ground testify that some small animals
must have passed through here.
name: A Stony Group of Hillocks

desc:
Thorny bushes that spread over the grassland are easy to bypass at daylight,
growing five to ten feet apart. Nevertheless, should a careless traveller run
into one of the bushes, the thorns stick to clothing and tear skin, making it
much harder to advance in low light. The brushland continues to the west, where
the bushes are starting to grow back after suffering in a recent fire.
name: Edge of a Brushwood

desc:
Coaled and blackened stumps of trees that once grew here are the only signs of
the fire. Brush has started to grow back and has hidden the wounds caused to
nature. Frequent cries rising from scattered bushes indicate that even birds
have started to claim back their old nesting spots. The effects of the fire are
more evident in the north, whereas the brush gets thicker and more healthy in
the west.
name: Burnt Brushland

desc:
The land here is completely overgrown with twisted, dark-green bushes, some
serving as a safe nesting place to various small birds. To the north, the brush
becomes thicker and taller, covering the horizon. Never-ending grasslands
stretch all the way west.
name: Flat Brushland

desc:
A carpet of green grass covers the land here, prevailing over other vegetation.
A lonely twisted tree bends to the ground, making a whistling and howling sound
as wind passes through its leaves and branches. A few blue wild flowers dot the
ground around the tree, thriving in its shadows.
name: Grass-covered Plains

desc:
The area here is covered by waist-high bushes, stretching their vines over the
grass in an eternal fight for sunlight. This is truly a place where nature
thrives. The brush gets too dense to pass through, to the east.
name: Edge of Brushland

desc:
A fire must have raged here long ago, yet nature has started to regenerate.
Fresh grass is growing on the now cracked and burnt ground and around blackened
tree trunks young saplings have sprouted. Once thick brush is now lying near the
ground, coaled and scorched by the fire, making passage through the burnt land
harder.
name: Burnt Brushland

desc:
Several huge boulders are sprinkled across the grass-covered plain and are now
half sunk into the soil. Even though winds and pounding rain have eroded the
stones, it is evident that they were once hewn into rectangular blocks. The
grasslands stretch for miles in every direction besides north, where a stone
quarry opens up.
name: Plains beside a Quarry

desc:
There is little vegetation in the sand. Most of the cutting weed that once grew
here has given up the fight in barren soil and has turned reddish brown. On the
ground lie dry, grey straws, still holding on to the soil with their dead roots.
Everything is dry and desolate, and scattered pieces of sandy wood and small,
smooth stones give it even more lifeless look.
name: Field of Dry, Grey Straws

desc:
The vegetation here continues its never-ending struggle for life, the only
survivors being low bushes and tufts of dark grey grass hiding in the shadows of
stunted pines. The plants are crawling near the ground, reaching out for every
little drop of water. The barren sandy soil is covered by a number of footprints
of little creatures, their tracks crossing each other and running in zigzag,
suddenly ending in small burrows.
name: Sandy Plains

desc:
The rolling ground is covered by low, yet thick grass, dotted with occasional
wild flowers. Some small knolls rise above the surroundings, providing natural
vantage points. From atop those knolls, an outline of a building is visible in
the north-west, whereas on other sides the plains continue without much changes.
name: Small Knolls

desc:
A small, clear stream swiftly makes its way from north to south through the
grasslands, almost knee-deep at its deepest point but narrow enough to jump
over. Green weeds growing at the bottom of the stream are clearly visible
through the water. Soft and sticky mud on either side of the stream is covered
with footprints of numerous animals, their tracks leading around the riverside
trees to lush grasslands stretching to the horizon on all sides.
name: A Small Stream in the High Grass

desc:
A massive oak tree overlooks the flat landscape of the lush meadow from high
above. Its almost eight-feet wide trunk is covered by a thick dark-brown bark,
hardened with ages. The unreachable lower branches start high above, providing
almost no support for climbing up. The nesting spots that the hollow trunk and
huge branches provide are well hidden from view. Sloping ground around the tree
is covered with a layer of rusty-coloured dead leaves and strewn with greyish-
brown acorns.
name: A Lonely Oak Tree

desc:
A faint footpath wanders through the fields in a north-south direction, turning
and twisting to avoid larger clumps of brush. The path is overgrown with low,
brown-stained grass and its sides are covered with various shrubs of different
sizes. At the south end of the path stands an old and gnarled apple tree,
stretching its huge moss-covered branches towards the sky. The thick dark-brown
bark around the trunk is cracked and some smaller plants have rooted there.
name: Footpath near an Apple Tree

desc:
In contrast to the rich grasslands to the west, the hardy weeds here are sparse
and grow in tufts, exposing poor soil and small stones. To the east the grass
grows thinner and the ground gets more rocky and barren as it slowly rises.
Occasional stronger winds pick up dirt and grit, making breathing harder.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
The trail runs through a colourful field, stretching out from west to east.
Glimpses of green and yellow grass can be rarely seen in the variety of wild
flowers growing in different colours and sizes. Most noticeable are the
cornflowers, with their intense blue heads, and foxgloves, which are in both
white and purple colour. Occasional yellow coneflowers reach out for the sun,
standing tall above all other plants.
name: A Trail in the Field of Flowers

desc:
A small circle of blackened stones have been placed in front of a decaying hut
north of the trail. Tall grass around the fireplace almost completely hides a
few larger logs sitting firmly on the ground. Once a favoured place to rest
during the night, this camp seems to get little use now. The trail continues to
the west and east, and a small footpath leads into the vast fields of grass
southwards.
name: An Abandoned Campsite along a Trail

desc:
The trail bends to the north and east here, twisting to stay on higher ground.
It is easy to follow and provides sure footing, though the overgrowth slows
progress. The trail is flanked with blackberry vines and other thorny shrubs,
and covered by thick grass. A small hut to the east contrasts sharply to the
wide plains stretching to all other sides as far as the eye can see.
name: Overgrown Trail

desc:
Over the green grasslands rises a steep, grey hill, strewn with stunted juniper
bushes which have been twisted and bent by raging winds. On the south side near
the highest point of the hill the bushes thicken, forming a dense tangle. Around
the foot of the hill and for many miles around there are no junipers to be seen,
as if they all moved together to the hill.
name: Stunted Junipers

desc:
The worn path makes a long bend here, staying close to the high walls of the
stronghold in the north-west. To the west the trail heads towards the gate in
the wall, but to the north it soon disappears among the plains. Some rough
boulders stand next to the path, but whoever placed them there must have lost
interest, as they are half-sunken into the soil and fringed with low shrubs.
name: Bend in the Worn Path

desc:
Atop of a height in the north stands an old, relatively massive structure
encircled with a wall. It seems like a fortified farmhouse or a small outpost.
The passing time has left its traces, and a plaque that had been fixed to the
wall has fallen down long ago. The only visible entrance to the simple
stronghold is an old gate made of thick hardwood planks, fastened together by
wrought iron studs. In the wall's western part, a small tower rises above the
fort, giving good overview of the gate and surrounding plains. The upper part of
the tower was once made of wood and has toppled long ago, now covered by
vegetation.
name: In Front of a Wooden Gate

desc:
The gently rolling plains here are lonely and dun-coloured, stretching as far as
the eye can see in most directions, with only occasional trees breaking up the
monotony of the land. In the west, however, an overgrown trail runs down a
debris-strewn slope to an old stone quarry.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
In the east side of a hill is an old stone quarry. Large chunks of granite have
been taken out of the hill and transported away, leaving only sheer walls with
drilling holes and other quarrying marks behind. The steep hill-faces with ferns
and brambles growing in their niches encompass the quarry to the north, west,
and south. In the east, a long inclined ramp with a grade gentle enough to haul
the stones leads out of the quarry.
name: An Old Granite Quarry

desc:
The room here is built into a natural cave, probably by the miners to keep their
tools and supplies. Some broken pieces of old mining equipment and rotten ropes
are still scattered on the floor, but most of it has been rendered useless by
time. The air is damp, and the heavy scent of mould and rotten wood is mixed
with the smell of moist earth. An old door in the south leads out of the room.
name: Abandoned Storage Room

desc:
Rolling grasslands give way to a barren slope rising steadily until it meets a
sheer cliff towering above in the east. The vertical wall rises almost forty
feet, providing no support for climbing up. Fallen rocks and boulders lie
scattered at the base of the cliff and the sound of a falling stone can be
occasionally heard. High nettles seem to be the only plant thriving among the
debris.
name: Rocky Slope

desc:
The lush meadows in the north and west suddenly end with a steep valley in the
south and a sheer cliff in the east. Rising almost forty feet above ground, the
vertical wall looks impossible to scale. The ground is filled with sharp rocks
and small pebbles, dislogded from the cliff towering above.
name: Below a Cliff

desc:
Overlooking a deep valley in the south, the grassy meadow spreads to the very
edge of the almost vertical rock face. Rocky walls of the old riverbed are still
smooth, offering no handholds for climbing down. In the north, however, the
plains continue in all directions, covered with lush grass and dotted with
occasional lone trees.
name: Meadow Overlooking a Valley

desc:
Besides the tall grass, reaching up to man's waist, nothing can be seen in a
northerly direction. In the south, on the contrary, the meadow is suddenly cut
off with a deep valley running in an east-west direction. A tiny fir tree stands
just above the steep face of rock, watching over the ground below like a shadowy
guardian.
name: Meadow Overlooking a Valley

desc:
A little cluster of thorn trees marks the top of a low knoll. All of its sides
are covered with huge granite boulders blocking the way up, only on the southern
side the boulders have been parted just enough to make room for a small trail
going up to the top. Man-high nettles grow among the rocks, interspersed with
patches of wild raspberry.
name: A Knoll in the Thick Grass

desc:
The shores of a small stream going through the meadow are filled with stumps of
fallen trees sticking out of the mud. Trunks reaching across the water are
covered with tooth marks of the creatures that cut down the trees. Near a bend
